title: So called issues with sexualization
wikitext:
[]: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Darth Plato » Tue Jan 27, 2026 7:52 pm I saw a comment in cyberspace where someone said they loved Evangelion but "hate how it sexualized children." I am sorry, but has this person watched ANYTHING American that has teenagers in it? It's beyond hypocritical how people act like Eva is "dirty" when US tv perpetually sexualizes teenage female characters, as do American films. When I think of this I am just baffled. I think it is somehow the fact that the characters are animated. Americans watch Married With Children and That Seventies Show and so on. These shows revolve around teenage sexuality or display female characters portrayed as sexpots. Eva has awkward situations and crushes (not counting the hospital scene of course which is extreme, but besides the point) but compared to films like Porky's or Fast Times at Ridgemont High, the "sexualized" content about Asuka and Rei is extremely tame. Why are Americans so hypocritical?

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Wed Jan 28, 2026 10:03 am I agree completely with the above. We should also bear in mind that standards on what is appropriate vary from country to country and region to region. Westerners should not try to impose their standards on Japan. Whenever I see someone on line complaining about fan service my reaction is: "If you don't like it don't watch it."

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Wed Feb 11, 2026 12:00 am Darth Plato wrote: Why are Americans so hypocritical?

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Deadly » Thu Feb 12, 2026 12:51 pm Why are Americans so hypocritical?

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Darth Plato » Sun Feb 15, 2026 11:16 pm I guess I should say that the context where I found the comment--sorry I don't remember--seemed to fit into a "Japanese people are perverts" kind of vibe that annoys me as a fan of Japanese narratives and media content. Yes there is "dirty" Japanese stuff, but I think Americans tend to perceive really marginal stuff like dirty-panty vending machines as somehow normal there, compared to a putatively "normal" sexual culture in the USA. I guess it bothers me that since there is lewd fan art about Eva characters, some people perceive that as somehow indicative of the purpose of the show or something. It's a thing with the Battle Angel Alita franchise too that people with little familiarity seem to think the whole thing is some sort of weird pornography or something, rather than a meaningful story. I'm not saying there CAN'T be anything wrong or problematic or even "wrong-ish" in any real content, even in Eva, but that I am annoyed at shallow outsider takes. The story of NGE definitely deals with teenagers and young adults who struggle with sexual tensions. There is also a kind of running gag about fan service in the next episode teasers. Misato is presented as a "sexy" character. But when the pilots deal with sexual situations or nudity on the show, it is eiher comedic or narratively relevant. For instance when Asuka leans in toward Shinji while explaining thermal expansion, she is clearly teasing him for attention. It is part of the character and relationship development. Or when Shinji falls on Rei in her apartment, that scene has a point. Shinji is in awkard situations and so are the girls. However, I don't see these scenes as exploitative--no more than any live action content featuring characters this age might be. Perhaps I am being obtuse. Or maybe I am just complaining to the choir. EDIT: Perhaps my angle on this will be more clear if I compare NGE to numerous Disney films, as a contrast. Some people think these movies portray an unrealistic beauty ideal, or that princesses with impossible bodies are problematic. Although sexual frankness is not part of the recipe there, such characters are often romantically preoccupied teenagers. The characters are drawn to be pretty and appealing. That doesn't mean Disney movies are meant to elicit arousal from the viewer even if the characters are proportioned as "sexy." The fact that NGE approaches issues like Asuka being infatuated with Kaji or dealing with menstruation is just part of it being realistic to me. Maybe some viewers get a weird rise out of it. But honestly, a person who has a thing for animated characters could have inappropriate fantasies about Disney characters too, and there are surely also lewds of those characters somewhere out there on the net. Perhaps I should clarify again---if someone is disturbed by the hospital scene in EOE or even the part where Shinji leans in to kiss Asuka on the floor, well that is a real conversation. What seems silly or hypocritical to me is if someone thinks it is wrong for characters to be drawn or portrayed in a way that viewers will understand them as pretty or whatever. In a show meant for people who are mature enough to process characters being attracted to each other as part of the story, some of that will be depicted where a character notices another. Since I have only seen the rebuilds once I might have missed something really glaring. I just think that the sexual references, sexual tension, nudity and so forth ordinary serves a purpose in NGE/EOE and it isn't meant to be pornographic as such. Most of it is comedic, and some is very serious but there is always a point.

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Wed Feb 18, 2026 8:05 pm Darth Plato wrote: I guess I should say that the context where I found the comment--sorry I don't remember--seemed to fit into a "Japanese people are perverts" kind of vibe that annoys me as a fan of Japanese narratives and media content. Yes there is "dirty" Japanese stuff, but I think Americans tend to perceive really marginal stuff like dirty-panty vending machines as somehow normal there, compared to a putatively "normal" sexual culture in the USA.

[]: Re: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Darth Plato » Wed Feb 18, 2026 9:40 pm I wasn't familiar with the term CSAM but I think I can figure it out. In this sense and perhaps some other ways I guess I wasn't being perfectly true to things I have read or heard. In general there has been permissiveness and a different attitude about sexual content in general, nudity, and specifically that involving young characters. Having noted this, apart from a video game I read about, Evangelion is pretty mild stuff when it comes to dealing with character sexuality compared with other titles. Of course, "normal" in Japan might be quite different from what is normal here. But the notion that Japanese people in general are into things like Urotsukuya Doji or whatever is not true. What is true is that prime time can have nudity and that kids' shows can have character deaths, and all sorts of things that wouldn't be acceptable in the US are acceptable there. The internet has changed some things and also invited international scrutiny and a push for common international standards. But I do think there was a weird phase in the 1970s throughout developed countries where lax attitudes about sexuality in general led to some stuff that is shocking to know about. So while Japan had Go Nagai's infamous "Scandal School" there were many live-action films in the US that crossed boundaries of morality and taste concerning characters who were minors. A major difference, as far as I can tell, is that some sort of pushback or backlash happened that circumscribed such content in the USA and this only came much later in Japan. Of course, Japanese notions of sexual mores have always been different from western societies. But here too in the matter of age, it wasn't all that long ago that girls were married off at 14 or even 12 in many western societies. Not as a rule, but it was accepted. I guess I have a thing about people trashing Japan. And I don't like it when properties with actual depth, such as NGE, are perceived as akin to other sorts of anime that are actually pornographic. I might not be able to strictly define the difference between fan service and porn, but as the saying goes, "I know it when I see it." An awkward or funny moment is different from... you know, ecchi or whatever.
summary: []: So-called issues with "sexualization"             Quote Post by Darth Plato » Tue Jan 27, 2026 7:52 pm I saw a comment in cyberspace where someone said they loved Evangelion but "hate how it sexualized children." I am sorry, but has this person watched ANYTHING American that has teenagers in it? It's beyond hypocritical how people act like Eva is "dirty" when US tv perpetually sexualizes teenage female char

In general there has been permissiveness and a different attitude about sexual content in general, nudity, and specifically that involving young characters. But I do think there was a weird phase in the 1970s throughout developed countries where lax attitudes about sexuality in general led to some stuff that is shocking to know about. So while Japan had Go Nagai's infamous "Scandal School" there were many live-action films in the US that crossed boundaries of morality and taste concerning characters who were minors. A major difference, as far as I can tell, is that some sort of pushback or backlash happened that circumscribed such content in the USA and this only came much later in Japan. But here too in the matter of age, it wasn't all that long ago that girls were married off at 14 or even 12 in many western societies. Not as a rule, but it was accepted. I guess I have a thing about people trashing Japan. And I don't like it when properties with actual depth, such as NGE, are perceived as akin to other sorts of anime that are actually pornographic. I might not be able to strictly define the difference between fan service and porn, but as the saying goes, "I know it when I see it." An awkward or funny moment is different from... you know, ecchi or whatever.
